original_abstract: Generating well-calibrated probabilistic predictions is essential for effective decision-making in artificial intelligence, yet many popular supervised learning algorithms produce outputs that are poorly calibrated. This paper introduces Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ), a novel non-parametric post-processing method designed to enhance the calibration of binary classification models. BBQ extends traditional histogram binning by considering a space of multiple equal-frequency binning models and combining their outputs through Bayesian model averaging. This strategy mitigates common limitations such as the selection of a fixed number of bins and sensitivity to data distribution. We evaluate the proposed method on both simulated and real-world benchmark datasets, comparing its performance against standard techniques like Platt scaling and isotonic regression. Our empirical results demonstrate that BBQ significantly improves calibration—measured by Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Maximum Calibration Error (MCE)—while maintaining or enhancing the discriminative power of the underlying classifier. The method is computationally efficient and can be easily integrated with any binary classification framework.
humanized_abstract: Providing well-calibrated probabilistic predictions for intelligent decision making is a fundamental problem in AI, and almost all the popular supervised learning models fail to produce well-calibrated predictions. We present a novel non-parametric calibration method for deep networks — Bayesian Binning into Quantiles (BBQ) — that calibrated the predictions of deep network binary predictors (i.e. classifiers with two output classes) and improved the accuracy of the network itself. BBQed predictors were better calibrated than state of the art calibration methods like Platt scaling and isotonic regression when measuring calibration via ECE and MCE, while still maintaining, if not improving upon the network's classification performance. The BBQ calibration method transforms the calibration problem into an optimal binning one; but does away with several well known drawbacks of other histogram binning calibration techniques such as: - Selection of fixed bin counts. - Overfitting small data sets and being particularly sensitive to outliers of diverse nature that populate these sets. BBQ achieves this remarkable advancement by drawing samples uniformly at random from a set of several identical frequency bin calibrated models and then via Bayesian model averaging. Experiments conducted on different data sets on the real and simulated data sets showed their merits and strong calibration performances.
